test.name: First parentage plan includes the parents' marriage record
test.description: flynn-first-plan: q_001 (who were Patrick Flynn's parents?) has no plan yet, and the only lead is Thomas as father from 1850 co-residence (indirect). A sound first plan for a parentage question must include a search for the PARENTS' marriage record — not just records about the child — because the marriage establishes the couple as a unit, supplies the mother's maiden name (which census and death records omit), and by its date relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth corroborates paternity (though a couple's marriage does not by itself establish that Patrick is their child). Pennsylvania marriage collections are available (collections-search-pennsylvania surfaces County/Civil/Compiled Marriages). Guards the record-type-guide 'Identify parents' improvement.

input.user_message: Create the first research plan for q_001 — who were Patrick Flynn's parents?

judge_context: The plan MUST include at least one plan item that searches for the PARENTS' marriage record (e.g., the marriage of Thomas Flynn, the hypothesized father) — an item distinct from records about Patrick himself. Pennsylvania marriage collections are available in the collections-search-pennsylvania fixture (Pennsylvania County Marriages 1885-1950, Civil Marriages 1677-1950, Compiled Marriages 1700-1900).

The marriage item's rationale should note its parentage value — at minimum that it supplies the mother's maiden name (which census and death records usually omit) and/or that its date relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth bears on paternity. Grade on the presence of a purposeful rationale that gestures at these purposes; an adequate rationale passes and need not enumerate every reason verbatim.

If NO plan item targets the parents' marriage record, score Record type selection as 1 (fail): omitting the marriage record is the specific gap this test guards. A present marriage item with an adequate parentage rationale is a pass (3).

The plan should also target the standard parentage record types (post-1850 censuses to track Patrick with his household, probate, church/vital records) — the marriage item is an addition to these, not a replacement.
